title: Navigating Order-(Dis)Order Family Trees via Group-Subgroup Transitions
authors: Shuya Yamazaki, Yuyao Huang, Martin Hoffmann Petersen, Wei Nong, Kedar Hippalgaonkar
abstract: As closed-loop materials discovery systems scale to produce millions of candidate compounds, the credibility of the novelty they reward becomes a critical concern. Novelty is commonly assessed against databases of ordered crystal structures, in which atomic sites are fully occupied. Yet, a predicted ordered structure may simply correspond to a particular ordering of a known disordered phase, whose sites are occupied by multiple species in the statistical average structure; we refer to such a structure as an ordered child of a disordered parent. Here, we introduce order-(dis)order family trees, a symmetry-based framework that organizes ordered and disordered structures through group-subgroup relations and enables novelty to be explicitly evaluated. We develop a high-throughput family matching procedure, to identify possible disordered parents and symmetry-related ordered relatives for a given ordered structure. As validation, we test our framework on synthesis-facing case studies (A-Lab), where it correctly recovers existing disordered parents for the targeted ordered structures. Extending this family-tree-based benchmark to experimental structure databases (ICSD), computational datasets (MP-20, Alex-MP-20, and GNoME), and crystal generative models further reveals that many ordered structures that appear novel as individual entries are, in fact, better understood as members of experimentally known order-(dis)order family trees. We also show that this is particularly evident in symmetry-agnostic all-atom generative models, which more frequently produce ordered structures derived from known disordered parents, whereas symmetry-constrained models are 2-4x less prone to this behavior. Our results establish order-(dis)order family trees as a key requirement for achieving genuine novelty in data-driven materials discovery.
summary: This paper addresses a critical flaw in automated materials discovery: the inability to distinguish truly new materials from known disordered phases that have undergone simple ordering. By developing a symmetry-based framework to map 'family trees' of structures, the authors demonstrate that many 'novel' predictions are actually redundant. They also provide a benchmark showing that generative models incorporating explicit symmetry constraints are much more effective at discovering genuinely new crystallographic lineages.
main_problem: Current materials discovery systems overestimate novelty because they fail to recognize that many 'new' ordered crystal structures are simply symmetry-lowered versions of already known disordered phases.
main_result: The authors introduce a symmetry-based 'order-(dis)order family tree' framework that reveals many computationally predicted structures are redundant members of known crystallographic lineages, and show that symmetry-constrained generative models are significantly better at avoiding this redundancy than symmetry-agnostic models.
method: A high-throughput matching procedure using the SWORD representation and group-subgroup relations to identify disordered parents and ordered relatives, validated against experimental (ICSD) and computational (GNoME, MP-20) databases.
model_or_system: The study focuses on crystal structures exhibiting occupational disorder and the group-subgroup transitions (specifically t-type relations) that connect disordered parent phases to ordered child phases.
key_observables: FT_disorder (fraction of ordered compounds connected to known disordered parents) and FT_order (fraction connected to known ordered phases).
important_parameters: Subgroup index [G:H], Wyckoff positions, and site occupancy.
main_assumptions: The framework focuses on occupational disorder rather than positional disorder and assumes higher-symmetry parents can be inferred by masking chemical distinctions.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the concept of a family tree using a Cu2GeS3 query; Figure 2 shows the recovery of known disordered parents for A-Lab targets; Figure 3 compares the performance of various generative models using FT metrics.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of inaccurate novelty assessment, presents the order-(dis)order family tree framework and SWORD representation, describes the high-throughput matching method, validates the approach using A-Lab and ICSD data, and benchmarks various crystal generative models.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Order–(dis)order family tree recovered from the query structure Cu2GeS3 (Imm2, highlighted in bright blue). Each phase is labeled by its space group symbol and SWORD label, with parent-child links annotated by the subgroup index. The vertical arrangement follows point-group order, with higher-symmetry phases placed near the top. In this example, one of the disordered phases a_c_216_S_{Cu+Ge} occupies the highest-symmetry position and acts as the parent from which both ordered and disordered descendants are derived through group-subgroup transitions. Ordered children that share the same parent define an order-order subfamily within the broader order-(dis)order family tree. Two...

Figure 2: Recovery of experimentally reported disordered parents for three GNoME A-Lab target structures. Ordered GNoME structures are compared with the corresponding disordered parent phases reported in ICSD and recovered by our framework for K2TiCr(PO4)3, KPr9(Si3O13)2, and Mn2VPO7. The colored labels at the bottom denote the elemental species, and elements enclosed by dashed outlines indicate disordered species.

Figure 3: Space group decomposition of FTdisorder across crystal generative models. For each model, FTdisorder is decomposed by the space group of the ordered generated structure, with P1 highlighted in red. The dominant contribution of P1 in all-atom models points to the possibility that some of the overproduced P1 structures originate as ordered children of known disordered parent phases. Right, an example of generated P1 child and its matched ICSD disordered parent in Fm¯3m.
